wines.name: Morgon
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, no new oak

taste.summary: Whole-cluster Gamay fermented with native yeasts, zero sulfur. Transparent, mineral Morgon with lifted red fruit and the characteristic schist-iron signature of the appellation.

pairings.why: The wine's bright acidity cuts through duck fat; cherry fruit mirrors the rich meat.
Classic Beaujolais bistro pairing; the low tannin and juicy fruit complement cured pork.
Delicate tannin and high acidity suit oily fish unusually well for a red.

history.summary: Marcel Lapierre began making wine in 1981, converting to natural methods guided by Jules Chauvet. The classique Morgon established the template for low-sulfur, whole-cluster Gamay that influenced a generation of Beaujolais producers.

milestones.event: First vintage under Marcel Lapierre label
Adopted Jules Chauvet's zero-sulfur fermentation approach
Marcel Lapierre passed away; son Mathieu continued the domaine

wines.description: Natural Morgon from Marcel Lapierre, the founder of whole-cluster, zero-sulfur Gamay in Beaujolais. Made since 1981 with native yeasts and old oak ageing.

wines.name: Morgon Sans Soufre
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, absolutely no sulfur additions at any stage

pairings.why: Zero-sulfur natural wine is the quintessential bistro companion in Lyon.
Juicy, light Gamay matches gentle white meat without overpowering.
Classic Lyonnais pairing; the wine's acid cuts through the rich egg yolk.

history.summary: The Sans Soufre release extends the domaine's no-sulfur philosophy to its logical endpoint, bottled with zero added sulfur at any stage. It is more fragile and designed for early drinking.

milestones.event: Sans Soufre cuvee first released for early drinking

wines.description: Zero-sulfur Morgon from Marcel Lapierre, bottled entirely without SO2. Drink young; the most fragile and vivid expression of the domaine's natural philosophy.

wines.name: Raisins Gaulois
wines.oak_regime: No oak; early bottled for freshness

pairings.why: Made for informal outdoor drinking; the low ABV and bright fruit make it ideal chilled.
The wine's simplicity and acidity work well with summery vegetable dishes.
Everyday Gamay suits casual pizza; low tannin is friendly with tomato acidity.

history.summary: Created as an entry-level Vin de France for early and easy drinking, Raisins Gaulois showcases the freshness of young Gamay without appellation weight. The name references the ancient Gauls and their love of the grape.

milestones.event: First vintage of Raisins Gaulois as a quaffer label

wines.description: Entry-level Vin de France from Marcel Lapierre, built for joyful, immediate drinking. Low ABV, zero oak, bright Gamay fruit.

wines.name: Morgon Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, extended maceration on old-vine fruit

pairings.dish: Roasted leg of lamb with garlic and thyme
pairings.why: Old-vine concentration and firmer structure match roasted lamb.
The wine's earthy iron notes pair with the nutty depth of aged Comte.
Schist-mineral Morgon has a natural affinity with earthy mushroom flavours.

history.summary: A selection from the domaine's oldest Gamay vines, this cuvee represents the ceiling of Lapierre's range. The old vines yield concentrated, structured Morgon with greater ageing potential than the classique.

milestones.event: Old-vine selection first separated as a distinct cuvee

wines.description: Old-vine Morgon from Marcel Lapierre, a selection from the estate's oldest Gamay parcels. Greater concentration and structure than the classique, with a 4-12 year drinking window.

wines.name: Morgon Cote du Py
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, 12 months minimum

taste.summary: Cote du Py Gamay from the volcanic-schist hill at the heart of Morgon. Dense, structured, with high acidity and an iron-mineral finish that rewards cellaring.

pairings.why: The firm structure and high acidity cut through marrow richness; the iron note echoes beef.
Gamey, earthy meat matches the wine's schist mineral and dark cherry core.
Classic bistro pairing; the wine's structure handles char and fat.

history.summary: Jean Foillard began making single-terroir Morgon from the Cote du Py in the mid-1980s, influenced by Jules Chauvet. The volcanic schist of the Py hill produces Gamay with unusual density and minerality.

milestones.event: First Cote du Py bottling under Foillard label
Domaine achieved full organic certification

wines.description: Single-terroir Morgon from the volcanic-schist Cote du Py lieu-dit, produced by Jean Foillard since the mid-1980s. A structured, mineral Gamay with substantial ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon Eponym
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres; small-production selection

pairings.why: The wine's concentration and firm structure match slow-braised beef.
Powerful washed-rind cheese needs a wine with structure and acidity to balance.
The earthy, dark-fruited character of Eponym pairs naturally with venison.

history.summary: A top-selection cuvee from Foillard's oldest Cote du Py parcels, Eponym is produced in limited quantities and requires extended ageing.

milestones.event: First vintage of Eponym as a separate top-selection

wines.description: Top-selection Morgon from Jean Foillard's oldest Cote du Py vines. Small production, maximum concentration, substantial ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon Corcelette
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres

pairings.why: The lighter body of Corcelette suits delicate potted rabbit.
Floral cherry-fruit Gamay matches classic bistro poulet.
The wine's acidity cuts through melted cheese richness.

history.summary: From the Corcelette lieu-dit in Morgon, this cuvee expresses a softer, more accessible side of the appellation compared to the Cote du Py. Named for the granitic-clay hillside parcel.

milestones.event: Corcelette first bottled as a single-lieu-dit cuvee

wines.description: Single-lieu-dit Morgon from the Corcelette parcel in Jean Foillard's range. Softer and more accessible than Cote du Py, with floral cherry-fruit character.

wines.name: Morgon 3.14
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres; blended from selected parcels

pairings.why: The everyday approachability of 3.14 pairs with comforting bistro pork dishes.
Juicy Gamay fruit cuts through duck fat in terrine form.
The wine's earthy notes match beetroot; the acidity balances creamy goat cheese.

history.summary: Named after the mathematical constant pi (3.14159...), this cuvee represents Foillard's approachable entry point to Morgon. Blended from multiple parcels for consistency and immediate pleasure.

milestones.event: 3.14 label first released as entry-level Morgon

wines.description: Entry-level Morgon from Jean Foillard, named after the mathematical constant. Blended from multiple parcels for everyday appeal and immediate fruit.

wines.name: Morgon Vieilles Vignes Cuvee Marylou
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, minimal intervention

taste.summary: Old-vine Morgon from Guy Breton with the characteristic zero-sulfur freshness of the natural Beaujolais school. Earthy, mineral, and age-worthy.

pairings.why: The wine's iron-mineral profile echoes blood sausage; the acid cuts through richness.
Delicate old-vine fruit complements rabbit without overpowering.
The mineral acidity of this Morgon works well with firm sheep's cheese.

history.summary: Named for Guy Breton's daughter Marylou, this cuvee is sourced from old Gamay vines in Morgon. Breton is part of the natural Beaujolais Gang of Four and produces wines with zero added sulfur.

milestones.event: Cuvee Marylou first released from old-vine Morgon parcels

wines.description: Old-vine Morgon from Gang of Four member Guy Breton, named for his daughter. Natural, zero-sulfur production with characteristic iron-mineral Morgon character.

wines.name: Morgon Saint-Joseph
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres

pairings.why: Floral cherry Gamay is the classic companion for roast pork in Lyon bistros.
Natural Morgon and cured meats is a quintessential Beaujolais pairing.
The wine's acidity and minerality suit the earthy, protein-rich Auvergnat pairing.

history.summary: Named for the Saint-Joseph lieu-dit within Morgon, this Breton cuvee comes from a specific parcel with heavier clay over granite, producing a more generous, floral style than the Vieilles Vignes.

milestones.event: Saint-Joseph lieu-dit cuvee first bottled separately

wines.description: Lieu-dit Morgon from the Saint-Joseph parcel, Guy Breton's more accessible cuvee. Natural production, floral and generous in fruit.

wines.name: Morgon Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres and demi-muids, very low sulfur

taste.summary: Classic Gang of Four old-vine Morgon: structured and mineral, with dark cherry and an iron-schist spine. Needs time to open but rewards patience.

pairings.why: The wine's earthy mineral character complements the long-cooked beef and marrow.

pairings.dish: Roast duck breast with cherry sauce
pairings.why: Classic duck and Gamay pairing; cherry fruit mirrors the sauce.
Rustic country terrine is the natural bistro partner for Morgon.

history.summary: Jean-Paul Thevenet, a founding Gang of Four member, has produced old-vine Morgon since the mid-1980s under Jules Chauvet's influence. The domaine remains family-run with the same minimal-intervention principles.

milestones.event: First old-vine Morgon under Thevenet label
Transition to fully organic and minimal-sulfur production

wines.description: Gang of Four Morgon from Jean-Paul Thevenet, produced from old Gamay vines with ultra-low sulfur. A structured, mineral Morgon with 4-14 year ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon Cuvee Breche
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres; a selection from a specific parcel with schist subsoil breach

pairings.why: Powerful game preparation suits the Breche's greater depth and tannin.
Firm mineral Morgon finds a match in aged Auvergne cheeses.
The raw mineral beef flavour has affinity with iron-schist Gamay.

history.summary: Named for a specific geological breach (breche) in the schist bedrock of a Thevenet parcel, this cuvee is a selection made in the best years from particularly concentrated old-vine fruit.

milestones.event: Breche first produced as a special selection in a standout vintage

wines.description: Special selection Morgon from Jean-Paul Thevenet, named for the geological schist breach in the parcel. Full-bodied, firm, and made only in excellent vintages.

wines.name: Morgon
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, whole-cluster fermentation

pairings.why: The natural Morgon's iron-mineral notes complement liver preparations.
Spiced lamb sausages pair with the cherry-pepper character of young Morgon.
Casual bistro pairing natural to the Beaujolais style.

history.summary: Damien Coquelet is the stepson of Jean Foillard and launched his own domaine in 2012, farming Morgon with the same organic, whole-cluster approach as his mentor.

milestones.event: Damien Coquelet established his own domaine, first vintage

wines.description: Morgon from Damien Coquelet, Jean Foillard's stepson. Whole-cluster natural Gamay from the Cote du Py area, launched in 2012 with the same organic principles as his mentor.

wines.name: Chiroubles
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, minimal intervention

pairings.why: Light-bodied, floral Chiroubles is one of the few reds that suits shellfish.
Delicate river fish matches the feather-light body of high-altitude Chiroubles.
Floral, light Gamay and goat cheese is a classic Loire valley-inspired pairing.

history.summary: Coquelet expanded into the high-altitude Chiroubles appellation, reflecting his interest in the range of terroirs across the northern Beaujolais crus.

milestones.event: First Chiroubles cuvee from Coquelet

wines.description: High-altitude Chiroubles from Damien Coquelet, a natural producer working across the northern Beaujolais crus. Light, floral, and refreshing.

wines.name: Morgon Cote du Py
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak foudres, 12-18 months

taste.summary: Structured Cote du Py Morgon from a reliable traditional producer. The volcanic schist soils give pronounced minerality and a firm tannic grip.

pairings.why: Structure and acidity handle the richness of marrow-enriched beef.
The smokiness of the duck resonates with the wine's graphite note.
Classic Burgundy stew is the natural partner for a serious Morgon.

history.summary: Jean-Marc Burgaud farms the Cote du Py with conventional but careful techniques, producing a consistent, traditional Morgon that shows the lieu-dit's schist terroir with honest clarity.

milestones.event: Cote du Py cuvee first bottled by Burgaud

wines.description: Traditional Cote du Py Morgon from Jean-Marc Burgaud. Volcanic schist soils produce firm, mineral Gamay with good ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon Les Charmes
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres

pairings.why: The softer, more approachable style of Les Charmes pairs well with classic roast chicken.
The gentle structure of this Morgon suits creamy potato preparations.
A classic Beaujolais bistro board with good ham and pickles.

history.summary: Les Charmes is a softer, more accessible lieu-dit cuvee from Burgaud, from parcels on clay-rich soil above the schist of the Cote du Py. More immediately approachable than the Py bottling.

milestones.event: Les Charmes first bottled as a separate cuvee

wines.description: Softer lieu-dit Morgon from Burgaud, from clay-rich Les Charmes parcels. Approachable and food-friendly.

wines.name: Morgon Delys
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, traditional Beaujolais semi-carbonic maceration

pairings.dish: Grilled pork chop with Dijon mustard
pairings.why: Cherry-fruit Morgon is the natural partner for pork at the bistro table.
Alsatian-style cream, onion and bacon tart matches bright, juicy Gamay.
Hearty winter soup warmed by Gamay's fruit and structure.

history.summary: Domaine Bouland is a family Morgon estate producing honest, food-friendly Gamay. The Delys cuvee is their entry-level Morgon showing the appellation's characteristic cherry and mineral character.

milestones.event: Delys cuvee first produced under Bouland label

wines.description: Entry-level Morgon from Domaine Bouland. Traditional production, honest cherry-fruit Gamay at an accessible price.

wines.name: Morgon Vieilles Vignes Cuvee Bellevue
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, 12+ months on old-vine fruit

pairings.dish: Braised veal cheeks with root vegetables
pairings.why: Old-vine concentration suits slow-braised veal with earthy accompaniments.
The wine's mineral density works well with firm Alpine cheese.
Duck, pork and white bean cassoulet is a classic Occitan match for structured Gamay.

history.summary: The Bellevue selection draws from Bouland's oldest Morgon vines on a well-exposed hillside parcel. Greater concentration and ageing potential than the Delys cuvee.

milestones.event: Bellevue old-vine selection first produced

wines.description: Old-vine Morgon from Domaine Bouland's Bellevue parcel. Greater concentration and structure than the Delys, with 3-10 year ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, natural yeast, no filtration

pairings.why: Natural mineral Gamay complements herb-crusted lamb.
Earthy mineral wine suits earthy root vegetables.
Classic Beaujolais pairing for natural Morgon.

history.summary: Mee Godard, who trained at the Beaune wine school and with natural-wine producers, established her domaine in Morgon producing organic Gamay with the principles of the natural-wine movement.

milestones.event: Mee Godard first vintage from Morgon parcels

wines.description: Natural Morgon from Mee Godard, produced from organically farmed old Gamay vines. No filtration, native yeasts, old oak ageing.

wines.name: Morgon Cote du Py
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, extended maceration, no SO2

pairings.why: Dark cherry and iron from Cote du Py soil mirror the cherry sauce.
The firm, mineral structure suits strong game preparations.
Mature cheese nutty notes complement the wine's schist-mineral character.

history.summary: Mee Godard's Cote du Py parcellaire is a small-production cuvee from the volcano-influenced schist of the Py hill, the named lieu-dit of Morgon.

milestones.event: Cote du Py parcellaire first produced by Godard

wines.description: Single-terroir Cote du Py Morgon from Mee Godard. Small production, natural, from volcanic-schist soils with no SO2 additions.

wines.name: Fleurie
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, natural yeast, no added sulfur

taste.summary: Natural Fleurie from Yvon Metras, with the appellation's signature pink granite-driven floral perfume. Zero sulfur and whole-cluster produce remarkable aromatic transparency.

pairings.dish: Roast quail with thyme and garlic
pairings.why: Delicate game bird complements the floral, perfumed Fleurie without competition.

pairings.dish: Carpaccio of beef with olive oil and capers
pairings.why: The light tannin and high acid work beautifully with raw beef preparations.
Fleurie's low tannin and floral register is one of the rare reds suited to salmon.

history.summary: Yvon Metras farms a handful of hectares of Fleurie with an uncompromising natural approach: zero sulfur, old vines, wild yeast. Production is small and demand exceeds supply.

milestones.event: First Metras Fleurie from old granite-soil parcels
Domaine fully converted to organic with no SO2 added at any stage

wines.description: Zero-sulfur natural Fleurie from Yvon Metras, produced from old vines on pink granite. Aromatically precise and widely sought after.

wines.name: Fleurie Le Printemps
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres, minimal maceration for spring-like freshness

pairings.why: The floral, spring-fresh character of Le Printemps mirrors the seasonal asparagus.
Lighter-bodied Fleurie suits fresh spring vegetables and gentle pork.

pairings.dish: Radishes with butter and fleur de sel
pairings.why: The most minimal of pairings for this light, aromatic Fleurie.

history.summary: Le Printemps is a cuvee expressing the fresh, springlike exuberance of Fleurie Gamay. Shorter maceration gives a paler, more ethereal wine than the classique.

milestones.event: Le Printemps cuvee first produced from Fleurie parcels

wines.description: Light, fresh Fleurie from Yvon Metras, designed for early drinking. The name evokes spring freshness and the minimal-extraction style reflects that.

wines.name: Fleurie La Madone
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, longer maceration from La Madone hillside

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with herbed crust
pairings.why: The fuller expression of La Madone suits a showpiece lamb roast.
Game bird with earthy accompaniment is a classic match for structured natural Fleurie.
The nutty, crystalline cheese echoes the wine's depth and length.

history.summary: Named for the hilltop Madonna chapel that overlooks Fleurie, La Madone is Metras's most structured cuvee, drawn from the steepest, most exposed granite parcels at altitude.

milestones.event: La Madone cuvee first produced in a warm vintage showing hillside depth

wines.description: Hillside Fleurie from the La Madone lieu-dit, Yvon Metras's structured hillside cuvee. Pink granite at altitude gives greater concentration and ageing potential.

wines.name: Fleurie Les Moriers
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, 10-12 months, traditional approach

taste.summary: Historic Fleurie single-vineyard from Les Moriers, one of Chignard's flagship parcels on pink granite. Perfumed, floral and classically structured.

pairings.dish: Roast quail with wild strawberry sauce
pairings.why: The wine's wild strawberry and rose notes echo small bird preparations with berry sauces.
Premium Bresse chicken in its own juices is the Burgundy-Beaujolais crossover match.
The floral, lifted Fleurie complements rich duck liver without clashing.

history.summary: Michel Chignard began making single-vineyard Fleurie in 1978 from Les Moriers, a hillside parcel on the granite soils of Fleurie. One of the longest-standing single-vineyard labels in the appellation.

milestones.event: Les Moriers first bottled by Michel Chignard
Son Gregory Chignard took over vineyard management

wines.description: Historic single-vineyard Fleurie from Les Moriers, produced by Domaine Chignard since 1978. Perfumed, granite-driven Gamay with a long track record.

verified.address_quoted: 830 route du Point du Jour, 69820 Fleurie

wines.name: Fleurie Cuvee Speciale
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, selected from older vine parcels

pairings.why: The wine's floral depth and acid cut through the richness of sweetbreads.
Game bird matches the wine's complexity; cherry note echoes the sauce.
The acidity of Fleurie handles soft, pungent washed-rind preparations.

history.summary: The Cuvee Speciale is Chignard's reserve selection, drawn from the oldest vine parcels on the estate and given extended ageing. It represents the upper tier of the Chignard range.

milestones.event: Cuvee Speciale first produced from the oldest Chignard vine selection

wines.description: Reserve Fleurie from Domaine Chignard's oldest vines, with greater concentration than the Les Moriers. Requires time in bottle.

verified.address_quoted: 830 route du Point du Jour, 69820 Fleurie

wines.name: Fleurie
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, traditional semi-carbonic maceration

pairings.dish: Grilled trout with butter and herbs
pairings.why: Light-bodied Fleurie suits river fish in the Beaujolais bistro tradition.

pairings.dish: Chicken in cream sauce (poulet a la creme)
pairings.why: Floral Gamay complements creamy chicken dishes.
Traditional Beaujolais accompaniment for the classique cuvee.

history.summary: Domaine Berrod was created in 1954 by Rene Berrod and is now farmed by Guillaume Ruet. A member of Vignerons Independants de France, the estate produces traditional Fleurie and Moulin-a-Vent.

milestones.event: Domaine Berrod established by Rene Berrod

wines.description: Traditional Fleurie from Domaine Berrod, established in 1954. Honest, food-friendly Gamay from granite soils with classic semi-carbonic character.

wines.name: Moulin-a-Vent
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, longer maceration for structure

pairings.why: Structured Moulin-a-Vent from Berrod has enough body and tannin for red meat.
Comforting Lyonnais pasta bake suits the wine's food-friendly structure.
Chicken liver terrine is a classic bistro starter matching well with this structured cru.

history.summary: Berrod's Moulin-a-Vent is produced from parcels on the manganese and granite soils of the appellation, reflecting the cru's capacity to produce structured, age-worthy Gamay.

milestones.event: Moulin-a-Vent production first established at Berrod

wines.description: Moulin-a-Vent from Domaine Berrod, produced from granite and manganese soils. More structured than the Fleurie, with 4-12 year ageing potential.

wines.name: Fleurie
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, traditional semi-carbonic maceration

pairings.why: Classic bistro pairing for accessible, fruit-forward Fleurie.
Chicken braised in red wine is the quintessential Beaujolais match.
Savoyard washed-rind cheese pairs naturally with the light Fleurie.

history.summary: The Despres family has farmed the hillside above Fleurie village for at least six generations. Their Fleurie classique is an honest expression of the pink granite terroir surrounding the iconic Madonna chapel.

milestones.event: Despres family estate documented at current Madone hillside site

wines.description: Six-generation Despres family Fleurie from the hillside above the village. Traditional semi-carbonic production, honest and food-friendly.

wines.name: Fleurie Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: Old large oak foudres, extended ageing from old-vine selection

pairings.dish: Duck leg confit with flageolet beans
pairings.why: Old-vine concentration matches duck confit richness; beans provide earthiness.
Delicate game bird suits the refined old-vine Fleurie style.
Semi-soft cheese with herbal notes complements the wine's depth.

history.summary: The Vieilles Vignes selection was introduced by the Despres family in the 1990s to highlight their oldest Gamay vines on the La Madone hillside. Greater concentration and length than the classique.

milestones.event: Old-vine selection first bottled separately as Vieilles Vignes

wines.description: Old-vine Fleurie selection from the Madone hillside, Domaine de la Madone. Greater concentration and length than the classique.

wines.name: Fleurie La Beguine
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, no sulfur, biodynamic farming

pairings.why: The earthy floral character of this natural Fleurie suits duck and root vegetable preparations.
Earthy Gamay with cherry notes pairs with mushroom-forward dishes.
Natural wine's food-friendliness shines with a simple charcuterie spread.

history.summary: Anne-Sophie Dubois launched her domaine in 2015, producing natural Fleurie from organically farmed parcels. La Beguine is her entry-level Fleurie, showing fresh, direct Gamay character.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Beguine under the Dubois label

wines.description: Natural entry-level Fleurie from Anne-Sophie Dubois, biodynamic producer. Cherry-fresh Gamay with no added sulfur.

wines.name: Fleurie L'Alchimiste
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, extended maceration, selected old-vine parcels

pairings.dish: Roast rack of veal with morels
pairings.why: The deeper, more concentrated L'Alchimiste suits richer preparations than the La Beguine.
Mineral, earthy old-vine Gamay complements wild mushroom preparations.
Firm mountain cheese pairs naturally with structured Fleurie.

history.summary: L'Alchimiste is Dubois's second, more concentrated cuvee, sourced from older vine parcels with extended maceration. The name references the transformational nature of winemaking from raw fruit to complex wine.

milestones.event: L'Alchimiste first produced from old-vine Fleurie parcels

wines.description: Concentrated old-vine Fleurie from Anne-Sophie Dubois. More structured than La Beguine, with 4-12 year ageing potential.

wines.name: Fleurie Clos de Bois Retour
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, biodynamic farming with Ecocert certification

pairings.why: Biodynamic Fleurie's purity of fruit matches herb-forward roast poultry.
Floral Gamay from granite soils pairs with lamb and fresh herbs.
Semi-hard mountain cheese suits the mineral, structured Bachelards style.

history.summary: Domaine des Bachelards produces Fleurie under Ecocert organic certification with biodynamic practices. Clos de Bois Retour is their flagship parcellaire from a walled granite-soil parcel.

milestones.event: Clos de Bois Retour first produced
Ecocert organic certification awarded to the domaine

wines.description: Biodynamic single-parcel Fleurie from Domaine des Bachelards, Ecocert certified. A walled granite clos delivering mineral, structured Gamay.

wines.name: Fleurie La Roche d'Onyx
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres, biodynamic, extended maceration

pairings.dish: Slow-roasted leg of lamb with anchovy and rosemary
pairings.why: The deeper, darker Roche d'Onyx suits powerful herb-anchovy lamb preparations.
Game meats find a peer in the wine's dark fruit and mineral structure.
The nutty, caramelised cheese notes contrast beautifully with structured Gamay.

history.summary: La Roche d'Onyx is Bachelards's deeper, more concentrated Fleurie, named for a granite outcrop of dark-coloured stone. Extended maceration gives greater structure and ageing potential.

milestones.event: La Roche d'Onyx first produced as a top cuvee

wines.description: Top-selection Fleurie from Domaine des Bachelards, from the dark granite Roche d'Onyx parcel. Concentrated, firm, biodynamic.

wines.name: Fleurie
wines.oak_regime: Old Burgundy foudres and demi-muids, biodynamic, no filtration

taste.summary: Burgundy winemaking precision applied to Fleurie Gamay. Biodynamic farming and old Burgundy foudres produce a mineral, precise Fleurie with genuine terroir expression.

pairings.dish: Chicken fricassee with tarragon and cream
pairings.why: Precision Gamay matches classic French fricassee without tannin disruption.
Low tannin and precise acidity work unusually well with cured salmon.
Mineral, acid-driven Fleurie suits Loire-style goat cheese well.

history.summary: Domaine Lafarge-Vial is a joint project between the Lafarge family of Volnay in Burgundy and the Vial family of Fleurie. The collaboration brings strict biodynamic vineyard management and Burgundy-style cellar precision to Beaujolais.

milestones.event: First vintage of Lafarge-Vial Fleurie joint domaine
Biodynamic certification achieved for all parcels

wines.description: Biodynamic Fleurie from the Burgundy-Beaujolais joint project Lafarge-Vial. Precision mineral Gamay in a Burgundy-influenced style.

wines.name: Fleurie Clos Vernay
wines.oak_regime: Old Burgundy foudres, extended ageing from single lieu-dit Clos Vernay

pairings.why: The Clos Vernay's greater density suits game bird with earthy accompaniments.
White meat cooked with precision suits a precise, mineral Fleurie.
Firm Swiss-style cheese with complexity matches the wine's depth.

history.summary: Clos Vernay is Lafarge-Vial's single-lieu-dit Fleurie, from a walled parcel on pink granite. The Clos format reflects the Burgundy influence of the Lafarge side of the joint domaine.

milestones.event: Clos Vernay first produced as a separate single-lieu-dit bottling

pairings.why: Light, silky Gamay with fresh acidity pairs classically with rabbit terrine.
Provençal anchovy-onion tart finds a fresh, fruit-driven counterpoint in Sunier's Gamay.
The wine's brightness lifts the washed-rind reblochon's richness.

history.summary: Julien Sunier started his domaine in Régnié in 2006, farming old-vine Gamay with biodynamic methods and vinifying without sulphur. His Régnié helped establish the appellation's identity among natural-wine enthusiasts internationally.

milestones.event: Julien Sunier founds the domaine in Régnié with 3 hectares of old-vine Gamay
Sunier's Régnié gains international attention in the natural wine circuit

wines.description: Julien Sunier's natural Régnié from old-vine Gamay farmed biodynamically: silk-textured, fragrant and minimal-intervention.

wines.name: Fleurie
wines.oak_regime: Old barrel élevage 10-12 months, no SO2

taste.summary: Sunier's Fleurie is a study in Gamay refinement: perfumed and silky with ethereal floral character, minimal intervention and an unusually long, airy finish.

pairings.why: Silky, light Gamay served cool suits the delicate white fish without overpowering.
Earthy wild cèpe mushrooms find a fragrant counterpart in Fleurie's floral register.
Young Époisses before peak ripeness matches Fleurie's lacy structure.

history.summary: Sunier acquired his Fleurie parcels in 2008, adding the appellation to the domaine's range. His Fleurie is vinified in the same no-sulphur, whole-cluster style as his Régnié, emphasising Gamay's perfumed, silky side.

milestones.event: Sunier begins farming Fleurie parcels and adds Fleurie AOC to the domaine range

wines.description: Julien Sunier's natural Fleurie: perfumed, silky Gamay vinified without sulphur, showcasing the appellation's floral and airy character.

wines.name: Wild Soul
wines.oak_regime: No wood; whole-cluster carbonic in cement vat

taste.summary: Wild Soul is Sunier's entry-level Beaujolais AOC: funky, crunchy dark fruit and wild-herb energy from whole-cluster carbonic fermentation in cement, no additions.

pairings.why: Funky, fresh dark-fruit Gamay is perfect against dry-cured sausage on bread.
Racy acidity and light body complement tomato bruschetta without clashing.
Light tannin and crunchy fruit hold up against the spicy heat of diavola pizza.

history.summary: Wild Soul was created by Julien Sunier as an approachable natural Beaujolais AOC without the terroir specificity of his cru wines, aimed at the natural wine bar and bistro market.

milestones.event: Wild Soul first released as Sunier's natural Beaujolais AOC entry wine

wines.description: Julien Sunier's natural Beaujolais AOC entry wine: funky, crunchy dark fruit vinified in cement without sulphur or additives.

wines.name: Régnié Exsultet
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 10-12 months, no sulphur

taste.summary: Ducroux's prestige Régnié from Demeter-certified biodynamic farming: deep cherry-and-clay concentration, wild-yeast complexity and firm mineral structure.

pairings.why: Firm tannin and mineral Gamay are a regional match with herb-roasted lamb and flageolet beans.
Clay mineral and iron notes in the wine echo the earthy lentil and smoky lardons.
Aged Cantal's hard texture and tangy acidity pair with the wine's structure.

history.summary: Christian Ducroux is one of Régnié's most committed biodynamic producers, holding Demeter certification. Exsultet ('rejoice') is his top cuvée, drawn from the oldest vines on the domaine's clay-granite parcels and fermented and aged without sulphur.

milestones.event: Ducroux converts to Demeter-certified biodynamic viticulture
Exsultet first released as the domaine's top parcel-selection Régnié

wines.description: Christian Ducroux's prestige Régnié from Demeter-certified biodynamic farming: deep, structured Gamay with clay-mineral concentration and no sulphur additions.

wines.name: Régnié Le Roc
wines.oak_regime: Old barrel élevage 8 months, no sulphur

taste.summary: Le Roc ('the rock') is Ducroux's granite-soil Régnié: lighter and more mineral than Exsultet, showing the appellation's fragrant, crunchy cherry character with a distinctive rocky freshness.

pairings.why: Bright Gamay cherry cuts the fat of classic pork rillettes.
Medium-weight natural Régnié works across a cheese board.
Mineral acidity complements the cardoon gratin's cheesy richness.

history.summary: Le Roc is sourced from younger Ducroux parcels on a rocky granite outcrop in Régnié, vinified in the same no-sulphur biodynamic approach as Exsultet but reflecting a lighter, more immediately expressive terroir.

milestones.event: Le Roc first released as the domaine's second Régnié cuvée at Ducroux

wines.description: Christian Ducroux's lighter granite-soil Régnié from Demeter-certified biodynamic farming: crunchy, mineral Gamay without sulphur additions.

wines.name: Beaujolais-Villages Domaine du Comte
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 6-8 months

taste.summary: Karim Vionnet's estate Beaujolais-Villages from the Domaine du Comte parcels: darker-fruited and more structured than a typical Villages, showing the producer's Morgon-influenced approach to low-intervention Gamay.

pairings.why: Medium-bodied Gamay complements the morel-cream sauce without heaviness.
Structured cherry fruit matches mustard-glazed pork roast.
Medium-weight Gamay pairs naturally with semi-aged Mâconnais cow's-milk cheese.

history.summary: Karim Vionnet set up his domaine in the Morgon and Beaujolais-Villages zones in 2005, farming with organic principles and minimal cellar intervention. The Domaine du Comte bottling uses estate parcels in the Villages zone.

milestones.event: Karim Vionnet founds the domaine in the Beaujolais-Morgon zone
Domaine du Comte cuvée first released as Villages bottling

wines.description: Karim Vionnet's darker-fruited Beaujolais-Villages from the Domaine du Comte parcels, showing a structured, low-intervention approach to Villages Gamay.

wines.name: Beaujolais-Villages Esprit d'Une Vie
wines.oak_regime: Old Burgundy barrels 10-12 months

taste.summary: Vionnet's prestige Villages cuvée from older parcels: Morgon-influenced depth and structure, with kirsch, incense and a long iron-mineral finish that punches above appellation level.

pairings.why: The wine's firm tannin and mineral structure hold up to long-braised beef cheeks.
Spiced earth and dark fruit complement cabbage-stuffed guinea fowl.
Blue cheese salt and creaminess contrast with the wine's iron-mineral grip.

history.summary: Esprit d'Une Vie ('spirit of a life') is Karim Vionnet's personal top Beaujolais-Villages cuvée, drawing from his oldest parcels and given extended barrel ageing to demonstrate the Villages appellation's overlooked depth.

milestones.event: Esprit d'Une Vie first released as Vionnet's prestige Villages cuvée

wines.description: Karim Vionnet's prestige Beaujolais-Villages from old-vine parcels: Morgon-like structure and iron-mineral depth in a Villages appellation wine.

wines.name: Beaujolais P'tit Caillou
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no wood

taste.summary: P'tit Caillou ('little stone') is Vionnet's entry-level Beaujolais AOC: unpretentious, fresh Gamay with crunchy red fruit and mineral granite lift for immediate drinking.

pairings.why: Fresh, light Gamay with goat's cheese on toast is a Beaujolais bistro classic.
Bright acidity and red-fruit profile echo the tomato's freshness.
Rendered pork belly richness is cut cleanly by P'tit Caillou's acidity.

history.summary: P'tit Caillou is Karim Vionnet's everyday Beaujolais AOC, produced without wood and released young for fresh, fruit-led drinking.

milestones.event: P'tit Caillou first released as Vionnet's entry-level Beaujolais AOC

wines.description: Karim Vionnet's everyday Beaujolais AOC: fresh, crunchy Gamay with granite mineral lift, bottled young for immediate drinking.

wines.name: Beaujolais-Villages
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 6 months

taste.summary: Romain Zordan's youthful-style Beaujolais-Villages: fresh fruit, bright acidity and minimal tannin for versatile bistro drinking.

pairings.why: Straightforward fresh Gamay pairs naturally with classic roast chicken.
Light tannin and acidity cut through country-style charcuterie.
Mild brie and lightly structured Gamay are a simple, satisfying pairing.

history.summary: Romain Zordan is a young producer in the Beaujolais-Villages zone, having set up his domaine in the early 2010s with a focus on clean, fresh Gamay at accessible prices.

milestones.event: Romain Zordan establishes the domaine and first bottles Beaujolais-Villages

wines.description: Romain Zordan's fresh, approachable Beaujolais-Villages from the Villages zone: light Gamay with bright acidity for everyday bistro use.

wines.name: Beaujolais Blanc

taste.summary: Zordan's Beaujolais Blanc from unoaked Chardonnay: light and crisp with white-peach and citrus-blossom freshness, clean mineral finish.

Citrus-mineral white Chardonnay pairs naturally with fresh herb-rolled goat's cheese.

history.summary: Zordan's Beaujolais Blanc uses Chardonnay planted in the granite-clay soils of the Villages zone, vinified without wood for a clean, crisp expression.

milestones.event: Beaujolais Blanc first bottled at Domaine Romain Zordan

wines.description: Romain Zordan's unoaked Beaujolais Blanc from Chardonnay in the Villages zone: crisp, light and mineral.

taste.summary: Domaine Forêt's Beaujolais-Villages: light, fresh and immediately drinkable Gamay with granite freshness, produced for the local restaurant and bistro trade.

pairings.why: Classic Lyon bistro sausage and lightly structured Gamay are an inseparable regional pairing.
Light body and fresh acidity complement the egg's richness.
Fresh quark-style cheese is a perfect match for light, fruit-driven Gamay.

history.summary: Domaine Forêt is a Beaujolais-Villages family estate producing approachable, terroir-honest Gamay from granite-sandy soils for the regional Lyon hospitality trade.

milestones.event: Domaine Forêt first bottles estate Beaujolais-Villages

wines.description: Domaine Forêt's light, fresh Beaujolais-Villages from granite-sandy soils: reliable, approachable Gamay for the Lyon bistro trade.

wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no wood

taste.summary: Forêt's entry-level Beaujolais AOC: light, crunchy and racy, built for drinking fresh from the year of harvest.

pairings.why: Crunchy, light Gamay is the textbook companion for a charcuterie spread.
Racy acidity and light body complement Alsatian-style flatbread.
Bright, fresh Gamay echoes the Provençal anchovy tart's aromatics.

history.summary: Domaine Forêt's Beaujolais AOC is made for immediate drinking, often released within months of harvest.

milestones.event: Forêt adds a Beaujolais AOC bottling to complement the Villages wine

wines.description: Domaine Forêt's entry-level Beaujolais AOC: light, racy Gamay for immediate drinking from the year of harvest.

wines.name: Morgon Côte du Py
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 10-12 months, no sulphur

taste.summary: Pacalet's natural Morgon from the volcanic Côte du Py hill: deep kirsch and iron-mineral concentration, earthy tannin and a long, volcanic finish characteristic of this iconic terroir.

Volcanic, earthy Gamay matches the gamey depth of slow-braised wild boar.

history.summary: Christophe Pacalet (cousin of Marcel Lapierre) works as a négociant-éleveur buying fruit and finished wine from trusted growers, vinifying without sulphur. His Côte du Py represents the iconic volcanic hill of Morgon at its most mineral.

milestones.event: Christophe Pacalet establishes his négociant operation in Beaujolais
Morgon Côte du Py first released as Pacalet's top natural Beaujolais cuvée

wines.description: Christophe Pacalet's natural négociant Morgon Côte du Py: volcanic, iron-mineral Gamay with no sulphur and structured ageing potential.

wines.name: Beaujolais-Villages

pairings.why: Fresh acidity and light body complement the earthy, nutty lentil salad.
Natural Gamay and local goat's cheese is a classic bistro pairing.
Light, crunchy Gamay is the default pairing for any charcuterie selection.

milestones.event: Beaujolais-Villages first released in the Pacalet négociant range

wines.description: Christophe Pacalet's natural Beaujolais-Villages: fresh, crunchy Gamay with wild-herb lift and no sulphur additions.

Volcanic-granite-adjacent cheese pairs naturally with a granite-soil Régnié.

wines.description: Christophe Pacalet's natural Régnié sourced from trusted growers in the youngest Beaujolais cru: fragrant, mineral Gamay without sulphur.

wines.name: Brouilly

taste.summary: Pacalet's natural Brouilly: clean, fresh red-fruit Gamay with granite mineral freshness and the lightness typical of Brouilly's sandy soils.

wines.name: Beaujolais L'Ancien

history.summary: L'Ancien ('the old one') was Jean-Paul Brun's statement about what Beaujolais AOC could be: old-vine, whole-cluster, no chaptalisation, no sulphur at harvest, fermented cool. The name references both the old vines and the old ways of making Beaujolais.

milestones.event: L'Ancien first released by Jean-Paul Brun as a demonstration of Beaujolais AOC potential
L'Ancien gains international critical attention for its old-vine complexity

wines.description: Jean-Paul Brun's old-vine, whole-cluster Beaujolais AOC from Pierres Dorées golden limestone soils: richer and more age-worthy than any typical Beaujolais.

wines.name: Beaujolais Blanc Chardonnay
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels and stainless steel blend

taste.summary: Terres Dorées' Chardonnay from the golden limestone of Pierres Dorées: between Mâcon and Chablis in character, with peach-blossom freshness, chalky mineral and restrained richness.

pairings.why: Cream-enriched Bresse chicken sauce matches the wine's gentle richness and acidity.
The wine's citrus-butter character echoes the classic meunière sauce.
Chalky mineral Chardonnay is a natural match for scallop sweetness.

history.summary: Jean-Paul Brun's Beaujolais Blanc draws on the unique golden-limestone terroir of the Pierres Dorées sub-zone in southern Beaujolais, a distinct expression of Chardonnay rarely seen at this appellation level.

milestones.event: Terres Dorées first releases the Beaujolais Blanc Chardonnay from Pierres Dorées

wines.description: Jean-Paul Brun's Côte de Brouilly from Terres Dorées: dark-fruited, volcanic-mineral Gamay from Mont Brouilly's blue-syenite soils.

Light, granite-mineral Gamay pairs with semi-dry goat's cheese.

wines.name: Morgon
wines.oak_regime: Large oak foudres 8-10 months

taste.summary: Château de Pizay's traditional Morgon from the Côte du Py zone: deeply coloured, iron-mineral and earthy with the Morgon-typical concentration and structure for medium-term ageing.

pairings.why: Classic slow-braised beef with Morgon's earthy concentration is a traditional Burgundian pairing.
Duck confit and wild cèpe mushrooms match the wine's dark fruit and earthy mineral depth.
Local Bresse blue cheese's salt and cream find structure against Morgon's firm tannin.

history.summary: Château de Pizay is a historic Morgon estate, one of the largest in the appellation, with a château dating from the 17th century. The estate has been bottling Morgon under the Pizay label since the 1970s.

milestones.event: Château de Pizay built near Villié-Morgon
Château de Pizay begins estate-bottling Morgon AOC under its own label

wines.description: Château de Pizay's traditional Morgon from the Côte du Py zone: full-bodied, iron-mineral Gamay with firm structure and ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: Old oak foudres 12 months

taste.summary: Pizay's old-vine Morgon from deep-rooted Gamay on the volcanic schist: kirsch, iron and leather concentration with the structured, long finish characteristic of aged Morgon.

pairings.why: The dark intensity of hare stew is matched by aged Morgon's depth and structure.
Roasted game birds with feather-weight sauce need the backbone of old-vine Morgon.
Blue-veined Fourme d'Ambert's creamy intensity needs Morgon's firm tannin.

history.summary: Château de Pizay's Vieilles Vignes Morgon selects the oldest Gamay vines on the estate, those planted more than 50 years ago on the deepest volcanic schist soils, for a richer and more structured expression.

milestones.event: Morgon Vieilles Vignes first released as Pizay's top Morgon parcel-selection

wines.description: Château de Pizay's old-vine Morgon from volcanic schist: kirsch, iron and leather depth with firm structure and long ageing potential.

wines.name: Beaujolais-Villages
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres 5-6 months

taste.summary: Château de Pizay's estate Beaujolais-Villages: more structured than typical Villages due to the estate's Morgon-adjacent soils, with a firmer edge and cleaner minerality.

pairings.why: The firmer structure of this Villages stands up to the rich confit gizzards.
Chicken liver terrine with structured Villages is a classic Lyonnais opener.
Young Époisses before peak pungency matches the wine's modest structure.

history.summary: Château de Pizay's Beaujolais-Villages is sourced from the estate's lighter-soil parcels outside the Morgon cru, offering an accessible entry to the house style.

milestones.event: Pizay first bottles Beaujolais-Villages as a second label for lighter-soil parcels

wines.description: Château de Pizay's Beaujolais-Villages from parcels adjacent to the Morgon cru: slightly firmer and more mineral than typical Villages Gamay.

wines.name: Morgon Jean Descombes
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 8-10 months

taste.summary: Duboeuf's single-domaine Morgon, sourced from the Jean Descombes estate on the Côte du Py: earthy, iron-mineral Gamay with concentration and the structure that distinguishes Morgon from other Beaujolais crus.

pairings.why: Earthy, structured Morgon is a classic partner for slow-braised beef and root vegetables.
Iron-mineral Gamay finds an echo in the earthy tomato-and-mushroom rabbit stew.
Volcanic cheese from Auvergne mirrors the wine's volcanic Morgon character.

history.summary: The Jean Descombes Morgon has been a flagship of the Duboeuf négociant portfolio since the 1960s, when Georges Duboeuf forged a long-term agreement with the Descombes family to select fruit from their Côte du Py parcels. The wine helped establish Morgon's international reputation.

milestones.event: Georges Duboeuf first selects and bottles Morgon Jean Descombes from the Côte du Py
Jean Descombes Morgon gains export recognition in the United States and United Kingdom

wines.description: Georges Duboeuf's single-domaine Morgon from the Jean Descombes estate on the Côte du Py: earthy, iron-mineral Gamay with firm structure.

wines.name: Fleurie La Madone
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 8 months

taste.summary: Duboeuf's Fleurie La Madone is sourced from the eponymous chapel-topped hillside: floral, silky and fruit-forward, the négociant's most recognisable expression of the appellation's perfumed character.

pairings.why: Silky, perfumed Fleurie is a natural partner for the region's premium Bresse chicken.

pairings.dish: Filet de veau en sauce crème
pairings.why: Elegant, floral Gamay complements the delicate veal cream sauce.
Light tannin and floral freshness mirror the Italian classic.

history.summary: La Madone refers to the statue of the Virgin Mary visible on the summit of the Fleurie hillside. Georges Duboeuf began selecting fruit from the La Madone lieu-dit in the 1960s, making this one of his longest-running single-appellation cuvées.

milestones.event: Duboeuf first selects La Madone fruit for a Fleurie single-vineyard bottling
La Madone establishes broad international distribution as a single-vineyard Fleurie label

wines.description: Georges Duboeuf's Fleurie from the La Madone hillside: floral, silky Gamay that has introduced the appellation to wine drinkers internationally since the 1960s.

wines.name: Fleurie Cuvée Flower
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 6-8 months

taste.summary: Cuvée Flower is Duboeuf's wider-selection Fleurie: accessible and immediately perfumed with rose-petal floral notes and soft tannin, widely distributed internationally.

pairings.why: Light, floral Gamay and leek quiche are a gentle bistro pairing.
Floral, fruit-forward Fleurie served cool is a natural salad accompaniment.
Rose-petal Fleurie with fresh goat's cheese is a quintessential Beaujolais pairing.

history.summary: The Cuvée Flower label was designed by Duboeuf as an accessible, widely distributed Fleurie for the supermarket and restaurant trade, emphasising the appellation's floral character in name and image.

milestones.event: Cuvée Flower first released as Duboeuf's volume Fleurie label

wines.description: Duboeuf's volume Fleurie: accessible, rose-petal-perfumed Gamay for everyday drinking, widely distributed internationally.

wines.name: Moulin-à-Vent
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 10-12 months

taste.summary: Duboeuf's Moulin-à-Vent from multiple selected parcels: structured, dark and mineral with the appellation's Pinot-like depth and firm ageing spine.

pairings.why: Full-bodied Moulin-à-Vent with firm tannin suits the rich, fig-glazed roast duck.
The wine's structure mirrors the red-wine butter sauce on the ribeye.
A firm, mineral Moulin-à-Vent pairs with aged Comté's crystalline salt and hazelnut.

history.summary: Duboeuf's Moulin-à-Vent has been a consistent part of the négociant's top-tier range since the early days of the house, sourced from selected parcels on the manganese-granite soils of the appellation.

milestones.event: Duboeuf first selects Moulin-à-Vent for his négociant range

wines.description: Georges Duboeuf's Moulin-à-Vent from selected parcels: structured, dark and mineral Gamay with firm tannin and ageing potential.

wines.name: Brouilly
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 6-8 months

taste.summary: Duboeuf's Brouilly from selected Mont Brouilly-area estates: accessible, medium-bodied and fruit-forward with the appellation's characteristic fresh cherry and clean mineral profile.

pairings.why: Medium-weight Brouilly complements the raspberry-vinegar-glazed chicken.
Approachable, medium-bodied Gamay works across a mixed local cheese board.
Rosemary pork ribs find a natural match in the wine's fresh fruit and medium structure.

history.summary: Duboeuf's Brouilly has been in the négociant's range since the late 1960s, sourced from trusted Brouilly-zone growers to produce a reliable, accessible expression of the appellation.

milestones.event: Duboeuf first bottles Brouilly as a regular cru in the négociant range

wines.description: Georges Duboeuf's accessible, medium-bodied Brouilly: reliable fresh-cherry Gamay from selected Mont Brouilly-area estates.

wines.name: Beaujolais-Villages
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres 4-5 months

taste.summary: Duboeuf's flagship Beaujolais-Villages: the textbook expression of the style, fresh, fruit-forward and accessible, sourced from dozens of Villages-zone growers across the appellation.

pairings.why: Duboeuf Beaujolais-Villages alongside charcuterie is a classic bistro pairing.
Light, fruit-forward Gamay with tomato and basil is an easy match.
Baked camembert with light, fresh Gamay is an easy bistro combination.

history.summary: Georges Duboeuf's Beaujolais-Villages is the négociant's largest-volume appellation wine, assembled from dozens of grower-partners across the Villages zone. It established Beaujolais-Villages as an internationally recognised French appellation wine.

milestones.event: Duboeuf begins négociant operations; Villages bottling becomes a core product
Duboeuf's Villages reaches export markets in the US, UK and Benelux

wines.description: Georges Duboeuf's flagship Beaujolais-Villages: the textbook expression of the style, assembled from dozens of Villages-zone growers for worldwide markets.

wines.name: Régnié
wines.oak_regime: Old barrels 6-7 months

taste.summary: Duboeuf's Régnié from the youngest cru: the typical light, fragrant and raspberry-driven character of the appellation, assembled from selected growers in Régnié-Durette.

pairings.dish: Salade tiède de pommes de terre au saucisson
pairings.why: Fragrant, light Régnié complements the warm potato-sausage salad.
Fresh acidity and light body cut through rabbit rillettes' richness.
Light, fresh Gamay with mild young Gouda is an uncomplicated everyday pairing.

history.summary: Duboeuf added Régnié to his range when the appellation was elevated to cru status in 1988, sourcing from selected growers in the Régnié-Durette zone.

milestones.event: Régnié elevated to cru status; Duboeuf first bottles the appellation in the same year

wines.description: Georges Duboeuf's Régnié from the youngest Beaujolais cru: light, fragrant and raspberry-driven Gamay from selected Régnié-Durette growers.

wines.name: Beaujolais Nouveau
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no wood; carbonic maceration

taste.summary: Duboeuf's Beaujolais Nouveau is the widely-distributed reference for the third-Thursday-November release: carbonic-maceration Gamay bottled and released on the third Thursday of November, with characteristically exuberant fruit and soft structure.

pairings.why: Nouveau and the autumn charcuterie harvest are a traditional seasonal pairing.
Light, fruit-forward Nouveau pairs best with fresh, mild cheeses.
The wine's soft fruit and gentle sweetness match warm apple tarte tatin.

history.summary: Georges Duboeuf did more than any producer to internationalise Beaujolais Nouveau, orchestrating dramatic release-day shipments to Paris restaurants and export markets from the 1960s onwards. The third-Thursday-in-November release convention was formalised by French law in 1985.

milestones.event: Duboeuf begins marketing Beaujolais Nouveau as a global event wine
French law fixes the Beaujolais Nouveau release date as the third Thursday of November

wines.description: Duboeuf's Beaujolais Nouveau: a widely-distributed vintage-fresh Gamay, released annually on the third Thursday of November.

wines.name: Moulin-à-Vent Château des Jacques
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels 12 months, 20% new

taste.summary: Jadot's flagship Moulin-à-Vent from Château des Jacques combines the manganese-rich granite soils with Burgundian barrel-ageing discipline: the resulting wine is Gamay at its most Pinot-like, with real ageing potential and deep mineral structure.

pairings.why: Full-bodied, firmly tannic Moulin-à-Vent is a natural match for dry-aged beef rib.

pairings.dish: Pigeon aux petits pois et lard
pairings.why: Pinot-like Gamay with dark fruit and structure suits roasted pigeon with petit pois.
The wine's firm tannin and mineral depth stand up to fully ripe Époisses.

history.summary: Louis Jadot acquired Château des Jacques in Moulin-à-Vent in 1996, bringing Burgundian barrel-ageing discipline to Gamay. The estate helped demonstrate that Gamay, when farmed on the right soils and aged seriously, can rival Pinot Noir in complexity and longevity.

milestones.event: Louis Jadot acquires Château des Jacques in Moulin-à-Vent
Château des Jacques Moulin-à-Vent earns critical recognition for Gamay's ageing potential

wines.description: Louis Jadot's Château des Jacques Moulin-à-Vent: Burgundian barrel-aged Gamay from manganese-granite soils with full body, firm tannin and long ageing potential.

wines.name: Morgon Château des Jacques Les Charmes
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels 12 months, 15% new

taste.summary: Jadot's Morgon from the Les Charmes climat within Château des Jacques holdings: iron-mineral, earthy and structured, showing the volcanic depth of Morgon with Burgundian barrel precision.

pairings.why: Iron-mineral, structured Morgon is a natural companion for slow-roasted leg of lamb.
The wine's earthy mineral notes find a match in porcini mushrooms with parsley and garlic.
Washed-rind Langres cheese's pungency meets its match in structured Morgon.

history.summary: Les Charmes is one of the Château des Jacques single-climat Morgon cuvées developed by Louis Jadot after the 1996 acquisition, applying Burgundian single-vineyard philosophy to Beaujolais for the first time at this scale.

milestones.event: Les Charmes first released as a single-climat Morgon at Château des Jacques

wines.description: Jadot's Morgon Les Charmes from Château des Jacques: single-climat, iron-mineral Gamay aged in barrel for Burgundian depth and structure.

wines.name: Morgon Château des Jacques La Roche
wines.oak_regime: French oak barrels 12 months, 15% new

taste.summary: La Roche ('the rock') from Château des Jacques is Jadot's most mineral and structured Morgon climat: stony, dark-fruited and firmly built, reflecting the volcanic rocky soils of this parcel.

pairings.why: Partridge and wild mushrooms find a complementary structure in this rocky, dark Gamay.

pairings.dish: Rôti de chevreuil sauce grand veneur
pairings.why: Grand-veneur venison sauce needs the depth and structure of La Roche.
Deeply aged Cantal's crystalline texture pairs with the firm, stony tannin.

history.summary: La Roche is the second Château des Jacques single-climat Morgon, distinguished from Les Charmes by its more stony, rocky soil profile and a firmer, more austere structure in youth.

milestones.event: La Roche first released as a single-climat Morgon alongside Les Charmes at Château des Jacques

wines.description: Jadot's Morgon La Roche from Château des Jacques: the rockier, more austere single-climat sibling to Les Charmes, with volcanic structure and stony depth.

wines.name: Fleurie Château des Jacques
wines.oak_regime: Old Burgundy barrels 10-12 months

taste.summary: Château des Jacques' Fleurie applies Burgundian barrel precision to the appellation's naturally perfumed Gamay: silky, floral and structured, more serious than the typical négociant Fleurie.

pairings.why: The truffle-under-the-skin Bresse chicken preparation finds a refined partner in structured Fleurie.
Silky texture and floral profile complement braised veal sweetbreads.
The wine's light structure and floral delicacy complement the alpine cheese's gentle richness.

history.summary: Château des Jacques' Fleurie was added to the range shortly after the 1996 Jadot acquisition, using fruit from select Fleurie parcels to demonstrate the Burgundian approach to the appellation's most perfumed cru.

milestones.event: Château des Jacques first releases Fleurie as part of the Jadot Beaujolais range

wines.description: Château des Jacques' Fleurie: Burgundian barrel precision applied to the appellation's naturally perfumed Gamay for structured elegance and longer ageing.
